trajectory_summary: The call is procedurally complete but emotionally thin. Bucky runs a clean checklist — address, title, utility, credit, booking — but never builds any energy around Nick's situation. The call ends with a positive arc (Nick asks about price unprompted), but that curiosity was generated despite the rep's approach, not because of it. The appointment is booked but structurally fragile: no co-owner check, no bill anchor, no button-up elements confirmed.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown. An appointment was booked, so the surface outcome is a sit. However, the appointment is structurally fragile: the co-owner question was skipped (anyone else on title is a required verify item), no bill was collected (new build, no first bill yet — lead type is ambiguous), and no button-up elements (one-hour block, computer requirement, 'is that fair' handshake) were confirmed. This should be tagged as a conditional booking, not a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky confirmed ownership but skipped the required 'Anyone else on the title?' follow-up. This is the upstream cause of the appointment's structural fragility — if a spouse or co-owner exists, the closer walks into a broken sit. It's a two-second question that Bucky omitted entirely, and it's the single highest-leverage fix on this call because it protects the sit before it happens.

key_moments.quote: That's what the house got just got built, and it's not on Google Maps yet.
key_moments.stewart_read: Nick's new build is a high-value signal: brand-new roof (no age concern), first utility bill not yet received (usage baseline unknown but clean slate), and a homeowner who just committed to a property and is likely thinking about long-term costs. Bucky registers the new-build context ('we just got billed') and correctly captures it for the closer — that part is fine. The actual miss is that Bucky never names this back to Nick as a curiosity hook. A new build is the ideal solar timing scenario (roof warranty intact, no retrofit complications, first bill about to arrive). Bucky had the material to say something like 'actually, new builds are the best time to look at this — your roof is fresh and you're about to see your first bill' and chose not to. Strategic hypothesis: Bucky was saving the curiosity hook for the closer. Reactive hypothesis: Bucky didn't recognize the signal's value. The reactive read holds — nothing in the call suggests Bucky was deliberately deferring this to the closer as a setup move.

Bucky confirms Nick is on the title but never asks the required follow-up: 'Anyone else on the title?' Per verify.title_co_owners, this is marked CRITICAL for spouse coordination at button-up. If a spouse or co-owner exists and is not included in the appointment, the closer walks into a structurally broken sit. This is not a strategic omission — there is no evidence Bucky planned to surface this later. The question was simply skipped.

key_moments.quote: And then you don't know how much you're paying in and all that?
key_moments.stewart_read: Nick has no bill yet — he just moved in. Bucky accepts this with 'Yeah. That's fine' and moves on. The bill anchor is dropped entirely. The schema requires capture AND emotional flip; here neither happens. However, the new-build context actually creates an alternative anchor: Nick is about to receive his first electric bill on a brand-new home. That's a forward-looking pain hook ('you're about to find out what this house costs to run — that's exactly when people want to know their options'). Bucky had the material and didn't use it. Lead type is also ambiguous here — if this is a paid lead, the absence of a bill is a structural problem for the appointment; if organic, it's preferred but not required. That ambiguity is unresolved.

key_moments.quote: Do you have a credit score over 670?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky states the threshold as 670, which matches the schema. Nick answers 800, then volunteers that 'next week it might be down just a little bit' — likely referencing a recent hard inquiry from the new home purchase. Bucky responds 'That's fine. That's fine.' This is procedurally acceptable but carries two unresolved issues: (1) the schema's spencer_operator_note flags that stated credit ≠ pulled credit and that setter DQ on stated credit is a policy question Kenny has not yet resolved; (2) affirming 'that's fine' on a stated 800 that the customer himself flags as potentially dropping is premature — Bucky doesn't know where it will land. The schema_drift check is clean (670 stated correctly), but the affirmation is overconfident given the open policy question.
